---
title: "Group Reflection"
---

## Skills inventory

For each skill area, describe what your group actually used it for. If you did not use a skill, say so and note why.

### Functions & refactoring (W2)

*What we did:*

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:* 

### Debugging (W3)

*What we did:* We fixed errors caused by missing values, undefined objects and plotting issues. We also checked model assumptions and inspected missing data.

*What worked well:* Functions such as `summary()`, `miss_var_summary()`, `vis_miss()` and regression diagnostics helped identify problems quickly.

*What was hard:* silent errors
and understanding a few unexpected or unusual values in the dataset took some time.

### Version control & remotes (W4)

*What we did:* commits, branching, and staging, basic commands as git status, pull, fetch, rebase, push, .gitignore

*What worked well:* no major or complex merge conflicts 

*What was hard:* 
We didn't encounter any major difficulties.
We could have used Git more consistently by committing smaller changes more frequently and using branches for larger or independent changes.

### Quarto websites & collaborative coding (W5)

*What we did:* We organised the project into R scripts that could later be integrated into the Quarto report.

*What worked well:*
Combine our analysis and results into a structured and reproducible report.

*What was hard:*

### R packages (W6)

*What we did:* We did use several existing packages including `tidyverse`, `ggplot2`, `broom`, `MASS` and `plotly`. We did not make new ones.
During the eda period, `moments`, `quantreg` were used. 

*What worked well:*
The available R packages covered all requirements of our analysis and made data preparation, modelling and visualisation straightforward.

*What was hard:* operating System between windows

### Initial data analysis & data cleaning (W7)

*What we did:* We inspected missing values, created new variables (e.g. delay rate, on-time rate), and aggregated airport-level data.

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:* Handling missing values and ensuring ratios were only calculated when flight counts were greater than zero.

### Reproducibility, open data & renv (W9)

*What we did:* We produced an renv file.

*What worked well:*
The use of `renv` helped to keep track of the required R packages.

*What was hard:* operating system between os and windows 

### Analysis & inference strategies (W10)

*What we did:* We performed descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, linear regression and Quasi-Poisson regression. Explore initial hypothesis with quantile regression.

*What worked well:* Testing assumptions before selecting the final regression model improved the reliability of the results.

*What was hard:* Choosing an appropriate model for count data and interpreting regression assumptions required additional research.

### Communication & visualisation (W11)

*What we did:* We created histograms, boxplots, scatterplots, regression coefficient plots and interactive Plotly visualisations.

*What worked well:* The different visualisations clearly communicated relationships between delays, airports and airlines.

*What was hard:* Design the plots 

## Key analytical decisions

Describe 2–3 decisions that had a meaningful effect on your results or workflow.

### Decision 1: Choosing Quasi-Poisson Regression

*What were we deciding between?*

Ordinary Least Squares regression, Poisson regression and Quasi-Poisson regression, Quantile regression

*How did we reason through it?*

The variance of delayed flights was much larger than the mean, indicating overdispersion. Therefore, a Quasi-Poisson model was more appropriate than a standard Poisson model.

I chose quantile regression during the eda section to explore initial hypothesis rather focus on the average effect since the dataset was highly skewed. And I considered it is more robust to extreme values and better suited for capturing relations.

*In hindsight, would we decide differently?*

No. The Quasi-Poisson model provided more robust estimates for our count data.

### Decision 2: Creating delay rate instead of only using total delays

*What were we deciding between?*

Comparing airports by total delayed flights or by the proportion of delayed flights.

*How did we reason through it?*

Large airports naturally have more flights, so total delays alone would be misleading. Calculating the delay rate allowed fairer comparisons.

*In hindsight, would we decide differently?*

No. Using both absolute delays and delay rates gave a more complete picture.

## Workflow & collaboration

*How did we divide work across group members?*

Each group member was responsible for a different part of the analysis, such as data cleaning, exploratory analysis, statistical modelling or visualisation. The individual parts were later combined into one project.

*What branching or Git workflow did we actually use — and did it match the proposal?*

We mainly worked on the main branch, with clear commit messages, so it was understandable what each person did.

*What would we change about how we coordinated?*

## LLM usage

Describe up to 5 significant uses of an LLM tool during the project.

### Usage 1: **Debugging R code**

- **Tool / model:** ChatGPT
- **What it produced:** Suggestions for fixing syntax errors and plotting issues.
- **What we had to correct or verify:** We verified that the proposed code worked with our dataset and adjusted variable names where necessary.

### Usage 2: Writing support, plots style

- **Tool / model:** ChatGPT
- **What it produced:** Help with improving comments and explanations for the final report.
- **What we had to correct or verify:** We rewrote parts to accurately reflect our own analysis and findings.

### Usage 3: Code refactoring

- **Tool / model:** Claude Sonnet 5
- **What it produced:** Refine repetitive lines, suggest lines to improve code readability 
- **What we had to correct or verify:**
